subject: english
chapter_title: The Magic Brush of Dreams (Mystery and Magic)

main_sections.title: The Magic Brush of Dreams
main_sections.poem_text:
Go and gather wild beans, Gopi,
Go and fetch some rice.
Go and pluck the mangoes
And bring home something nice.

Gopi sits beneath the banyan,
A twig is in her hand.
She sits there drawing pictures,
Pictures in the sand.

The winds blow by and sweeps away
The pictures in the sand.
But on a stone there sits a man,
A brush is in his hand.

He looks around. He calls to Gopi.
"Come here!" he whispers. "Hush!
We mustn't let the village know
About this magic brush."

He slips the brush into her hand
And tells her to be sure,
"Paint not for the wealthy ones,
But only for the poor."

"Did you gather wild beans, Gopi?
Did you fetch some rice?
Did you pluck the mangoes
And bring home something nice?"

"No beans, no rice, no mangoes!"
Gopi grins and runs inside.
She paints a bowl of khichdi
and waits,
Until the paint has dried.

"The bowl is full of khichdi, Gopi!"
Into the house the villagers streamed
The young and old all wish to see
The magic brush of dreams.

She paints a muffler for a farmer,
A plough for another man,
A shawl for a grandmother,
And for a girl, a fan.

And soon the news spreads far and wide,
Through forests, fields, and plains,
Until the Zamindar himself
Hears of these magic gains.

"I order you to paint for me,
A fortress tall and grand.
Paint treasures, jewels, and riches rare,
Enough to rule this land."

Gopi bows and shakes her head.
"My lord, I can't comply.
I swore to use this magic brush
To help the needy by."

The Zamindar roars and stamps his foot.
He bellows to his men,
"Seize this brush and seize the girl.
Throw her in the pen!"

Now Gopi waits inside the cell,
Upon a cold mud floor.
Until the Zamindar returns
And shouts through the door.

He holds the brush. He thunders loud,
"Paint my fortress tall!
Paint gold and rubies, emeralds bright,
And you shall leave this hall!"

That night the Zamindar lies in bed,
Dreaming of his gold,
While Gopi paints a winding road,
A horse both strong and bold.

The road leads out of prison gates.
The horse, she swiftly urges
Gopi leaps on and gallops fast,
Through fields and many villages.

"It's Gopi! It's Gopi! She's back again!"
Say the villagers, merry and kind.
But Gopi keeps on painting still,
With the Zamindar's men following
behind.

She paints a mighty river wide,
Its currents fast and deep.
The Zamindar and all his men
Stop short and cannot leap.

"Now should I paint a beast
To leap and chase you through?
Stop this chase or it will feast
On you, and you and you!"

The Zamindar cries, "No, no, no more!
I'll leave, I'll go away!"
He turns and flees; his men behind
Dare not choose to stay.

Now Gopi paints for the village folk,
A feast of sweets and tea.
A courtyard bright, a band to play,
And songs of victory.

Adapted from a Folk Tale
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: Adapted from a folk tale, 'The Magic Brush of Dreams' is a narrative poem in ballad form, following a predominantly ABCB rhyme scheme that lends it a rhythmic, story-like momentum. The central figure, Gopi, is a young girl who receives a magic paintbrush with the condition that she use it only for the poor. Her defiance of the greedy Zamindar—who imprisons her and demands she paint riches for him—forms the dramatic climax. Gopi's ingenuity and moral courage are the poem's core themes: she paints not weapons but a road, a horse, a river, and the threat of a beast to outwit authority and protect the village. The dominant imagery is sensory and earthy—bowls of khichdi, a winding road, a mighty river—reinforcing the poem's folk-tale roots. The magic brush symbolises the transformative power of art and imagination in service of justice, while the Zamindar's fortress and gold represent greed and oppression. The tone shifts from playful and innocent (Gopi drawing in sand) to tense and confrontational (imprisonment), and finally to triumphant and celebratory (victory feast). Repetition, as in 'On you, and you and you!', builds dramatic tension and reinforces the universality of the threat against the oppressor.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Do these activities before we read: I. Work in pairs. Discuss: 1. Mention a magical object you would like to have. Give a reason. 2. What would you draw if you had a magic paint brush? Why? 3. Would you use the magic paint brush for someone else? If yes, why? If no, why not? II. Discuss instances of acts of kindness you have heard, read, or experienced. III. Work in pairs and guess the meanings of the words given below and draw pictures: paintbrush, joy, fortress, feast, fear, winding, twig, bows, drawing, cold. Then arrange the words into categories: Things, Actions, Emotions.
Arrange the events in order of occurrence in the poem. The last one has been marked for you.

question_bank.stem: Arrange in order: 1. Gopi paints a bowl of khichdi which becomes real. 2. The Zamindar orders Gopi to paint treasures for him. 3. Gopi is told to gather food but sits drawing pictures in sand. 4. Gopi returns to the village with the Zamindar chasing her. 5. The Zamindar throws Gopi into prison. 6. Gopi paints a road and horse to escape. 7. Gopi uses the magic brush to paint useful items for villagers. 8. Gopi paints a wide river stopping the Zamindar. 9. A man gives a magic brush to Gopi to paint only for the poor. 10. Gopi scares the Zamindar away and celebrates with the villagers. [10 is last]

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Fill in the blanks by choosing the correct answer from the box: triumphant and celebratory / tension and suspense / playful and innocent. The tone of the poem shifts throughout, reflecting the different moods of the story.

question_bank.stem: Initially, the tone is 1. _________________ as Gopi uses her imagination to draw in the sand, and later when the Zamindar enters the story, the tone shifts to one of 2. _________________. In the final part of the poem, the tone becomes 3. _________________ as Gopi escapes, the Zamindar is defeated, and the villagers rejoice.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Fill in the blanks by choosing the correct option from within the brackets.

question_bank.stem: The poem mostly follows a simple rhyme scheme, _________________, which adds a rhythmic flow. (ABCB, ABBA, ABAB)
This poem belongs to the _________________ poetry genre, which tells a story through verse. (descriptive, narrative or ballad, free style)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Give examples from the poem for the following explanations.

question_bank.stem: The poem is rich in imagery, creating clear pictures in the reader's mind. For example, ___________________.
What is the effect of repetition in the line—'On you, and you and you!'?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Match the phrases from the poem in Column 1 with what they symbolise in Column 2.

question_bank.stem: Match: 1. The magic paintbrush / 2. The grand fortress / 3. The river and the beast — with: (i) greed and the desire for wealth / (ii) barriers to protect Gopi from the Zamindar's anger / (iii) the power of art and imagination to change the world.
question_bank.table:
Phrase|Symbolises
The magic paintbrush|(i) greed and the desire for wealth
The grand fortress|(ii) barriers to protect Gopi from the Zamindar's anger
The river and the beast|(iii) the power of art and imagination to change the world

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Identify whether the following statement is true or false. While Gopi uses the magic brush to help others, the Zamindar wants to use it for personal gain. This contrast drives the moral of the story.

question_bank.stem: While Gopi uses the magic brush to help others, the Zamindar wants to use it for personal gain. This contrast drives the moral of the story.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow. Extract 1: 'But on a stone there sits a man, / A brush is in his hand. / He looks around. He calls to Gopi. / "Come here!" he whispers. "Hush! / We mustn't let the village know / About this magic brush." / He slips the brush into her hand / And tells her to be sure, / "Paint not for the wealthy ones, / But only for the poor."'

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blank: The man is whispering and telling Gopi to keep the brush a secret reflects his _______________________ (careful and secretive / magical and delightful) nature.
What is the significance of the man slipping the brush into Gopi's hand?
Complete the following sentence with a suitable reason. The most likely reason the man instructs Gopi to 'Paint not for wealthy ones but only for the poor' is because he _______________________.
Select the most appropriate title for the extract: A. The Magic Brush's Secret  B. Gopi's Special Gift.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the given extract and answer the questions. Extract 2: 'And soon the news spreads far and wide, / Through forests, fields, and plains, / Until the Zamindar himself / Hears of these magic gains. / "I order you to paint for me, / A fortress tall and grand. / Paint treasures, jewels, and riches rare, / Enough to rule this land."'

question_bank.stem: Complete the following sentence: The news of Gopi's magic brush spreads 'through forests, fields, and plains' because of its _______________________.
Why does the poet refer to things painted by Gopi's paintbrush as 'magic gains'?

Which of the following statements is false? Rectify the false statement. A. Gopi's magic brush draws the attention of the powerful Zamindar. B. The Zamindar requests Gopi to paint a grand fortress and jewels.
What does the Zamindar's demand for a grand fortress and jewels reveal about his character?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Explain why Gopi made the right decision by refusing to paint for the Zamindar.
Gopi threatens to draw a beast to scare away the Zamindar. Justify her action. How would you have dealt with the situation differently?
What is the significance of Gopi celebrating with the villagers at the end of the poem?
Compare Gopi's use of the magic brush to how the Zamindar would have used it.
What can be inferred about Gopi's character based on her refusal to follow the Zamindar's orders?
Justify the appropriateness of the title of the poem.
If you had the magic brush, how would you use it in today's world?

listening.transcript_text:
Speaker (i): If I could have any magical power, I'd choose the ability to predict the future. Imagine being able to see profits and losses before time. I could make better savings and secure my business. Plus, I'd use it to help others make wise financial decisions as well.
Speaker (ii): I think having the power to learn instantly would be amazing! Exams would be easy, and I'd have more time for activities I enjoy, like playing cricket. If I could remember everything, it would make life so much easier!
Speaker (iii): I would want the power of flying. Imagine being able to see the world from up above clearly. I would be able to go from one place to another quickly without using any means of transport. It would be a dream come true.
Speaker (iv): At my age, I'd love to have the power of healing. There are so many aches and pains that come with growing old, but it's not just for me. If I could heal others too, I'd help my family stay healthy and happy.
Speaker (v): If I had a magical power, I'd want control over the weather. We depend so much on the rains. With this power, I could ensure that crops always get the right amount of rain and sunshine.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to five speakers speak about a magical power they wish for. As you listen, match statements 1–7 with speakers (i)–(v). There are two statements you do not need.

question_bank.stem: Match each statement with the correct speaker. Statements: 1. Power of healing ailments / 2. Ability to end poverty in the world / 3. Power to control the weather as per need / 4. Ability to make wise business decisions / 5. Power to predict one's own future clearly / 6. Ability to remember information quickly / 7. Power to move around without using vehicles. Speakers: (i)–(v).
question_bank.table:
Statement|Speaker
Power of healing ailments|__
Ability to end poverty in the world|__ (extra)
Power to control the weather as per need|__
Ability to make wise business decisions|__
Power to predict one's own future clearly|__
Ability to remember information quickly|__
Power to move around without using vehicles|__ (extra)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Intonation is the rise and fall of the pitch of tone while speaking. The 'falling' tone is used in imperative sentences. Read the following sentences from the text with the falling tone: 'Come here!', 'Seize this magic brush…', 'Paint my fortress tall!', 'Go and fetch some rice.'

Work in pairs. Take turns to make requests for the following formal and informal situations. Formal: 1. Request teacher to organise a class picnic. 2. Request teacher for an extra sports period since you missed it last week. 3. Request Activity incharge teacher to include you in the upcoming inter-school event. Informal: 1. Request sibling to help with completion of your project. 2. Request mother to enroll you in a dance class. 3. Request cousin to come over to spend the weekend with you. Use given phrases for formal and informal requests.

Write an imaginative essay on any one of the following. (A sample imaginative essay 'My Life as EcoFlash' and a checklist for what an imaginative essay includes are provided.) 1. Imagine you were a scarf that flew away from a clothesline. Describe the places you travelled to and how you came back. Also write about what you thought was the most valuable experience. 2. Imagine you stumbled upon a magical book in the library. The book transported you to a different time period. Describe your experience and an important learning.
The verb 'draw' has different meanings in different contexts. Match the sentences in Column 1 with their appropriate meanings in Column 2.

question_bank.stem: Match: 1. His speech drew loud applause from the audience. / 2. The event will draw huge crowds. / 3. Plants draw water from the soil. / 4. I reached the station when the train was drawing into the station. / 5. She drew the curtains. / 6. He drew a piece of paper from the folder. — with meanings: (i) opened / (ii) pulled out / (iii) got a reaction / (iv) attract / (v) moving / (vi) absorb.
question_bank.table:
Sentence|Meaning
His speech drew loud applause from the audience.|(i) opened
The event will draw huge crowds from all neighbouring places.|(ii) pulled out
Plants draw water from the soil.|(iii) got a reaction
I reached the station when the train was drawing into the station.|(iv) attract
The room was dark, so she drew the curtains to let the sunlight in.|(v) moving
He drew a piece of paper from the folder.|(vi) absorb

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Fill in the crossword. Clues from the poem are given (Across: gallops, surge, whispers, snatch, wealthy, seize; Down: flees, mighty, bellows, comply, streamed, victory).

There are certain expressions that convey a sudden feeling or reaction (interjections). Match the expressions in Column 1 with the emotions or reactions they convey in Column 2. Then create sentences using any five expressions.

question_bank.stem: Match interjections with emotions: 1. Phew! / 2. Eek! / 3. Ouch! / 4. Yippee! / 5. Whoa! / 6. Oops! / 7. Hey! — with: (i) surprise / (ii) pain / (iii) mistake / (iv) attract attention / (v) relief / (vi) delight / (vii) alarm.
question_bank.table:
Interjection|Emotion
Phew!|(i) surprise
Eek!|(ii) pain
Ouch!|(iii) mistake
Yippee!|(iv) attract attention
Whoa!|(v) relief
Oops!|(vi) delight
Hey!|(vii) alarm

question_bank.stem: Create sentences using any five expressions from Column 1.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Have you heard of India's famous magician, P. C. Sorcar Sr.? He is known as one of the 'World's Greatest Magicians' and performed live magic shows internationally. He was awarded Padma Shri on 26 January 1964.

Words can also play tricks. Read the riddles about things that seem to be unreal and find what they refer to: 1. I can fill a room, but I take up no space. I'm often heard, but never seen. What am I? 2. The more you take, the more you leave behind. What am I? 3. I have cities but no houses, I have forests but no trees. What am I? 4. I'm tall when I'm young and short when I'm old, in a dark room, my story is told.

question_bank.stem: Solve the riddles: 1. I can fill a room, but I take up no space. I'm often heard, but never seen. 2. The more you take, the more you leave behind. 3. I have cities but no houses, I have forests but no trees. 4. I'm tall when I'm young and short when I'm old, in a dark room, my story is told.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You are asked to create a magical object. What object would you create? Make a drawing with all its details and mention its special quality on the same sheet. Put up the sheet on the display board.
